section.title: One Sacrifice Once for All
section.sectionRationale: The writer brings the priestly argument to its climax. The law's endlessly repeated sacrifices could never take away sins or perfect the worshiper; they were only a shadow. Christ came to do God's will, offering His body once for all. By that single offering He has perfected forever those being sanctified, and where sins are forgiven, no further offering is needed.
section.thesis: The law's repeated sacrifices, being only a shadow, could never take away sins or perfect the worshiper; but Christ came to do God's will and by the single offering of His body has sanctified His people and perfected forever those being sanctified, so that, sins being forgiven, no further sacrifice for sin remains.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the law's shadow and its powerless, repeated sacrifices; Christ's coming to do God's will, offering His body once for all; and the finality of that one offering, which perfects forever and, with forgiveness secured, ends all sacrifice for sin.

verses.text: For the law is only a {{skia|shadow}} of the good things to come, not the realities themselves. It can never, by the same sacrifices offered year after year, make perfect those who draw near to worship.
If it could, would not the offerings have ceased? For the worshipers would have been cleansed once for all, and would no longer have felt the guilt of their sins.
Instead, those sacrifices are an annual reminder of sins,
because it is impossible for the blood of bulls and goats to take away sins.

units.label: A body You prepared for Me
units.note: I have come to do Your will

verses.text: Therefore, when Christ came into the world, He said: “Sacrifice and offering You did not desire, but a body You prepared for Me.
In burnt offerings and sin offerings You took no delight.
Then I said, ‘Here I am, it is written about Me in the scroll: I have come to do Your will, O God.’”
In the passage above He says, “Sacrifices and offerings, burnt offerings and sin offerings You did not desire, nor did You delight in them” (although they are offered according to the law).
Then He adds, “Here I am, I have come to do Your will.” He takes away the first to establish the second.
And by that will, we have been {{hagiazo|sanctified}} through the sacrifice of the body of Jesus Christ {{ephapax|once for all}}.
Day after day every priest stands to minister and to offer again and again the same sacrifices, which can never take away sins.
But when this Priest had offered for all time one sacrifice for sins, He sat down at the right hand of God.
Since that time, He waits for His enemies to be made a footstool for His feet,
because by a single offering He has made {{teleioo|perfect}} for all time those who are being sanctified.
The Holy Spirit also testifies to us about this. First He says:
“This is the covenant I will make with them after those days, declares the Lord. I will put My laws in their hearts and inscribe them on their minds.”
Then He adds: “Their sins and lawless acts I will remember no more.”
And where these have been forgiven, an offering for sin is no longer needed.

skia.gloss: A shadow, the faint outline cast by a coming reality. The law was only a shadow of the good things to come, not the substance; its repeated sacrifices could sketch but never supply the true removal of sins.

hagiazo.gloss: To sanctify, to make holy, to set apart for God. By God's will, believers have been sanctified through the once-for-all offering of Christ's body, both decisively set apart and progressively made holy.

ephapax.gloss: Once for all, at a single decisive moment never to be repeated. Christ's body was offered once for all, in stark contrast to the daily and yearly sacrifices that had to be endlessly repeated.

teleioo.gloss: To make perfect, complete, fully fit to draw near to God. By a single offering Christ has perfected forever those being sanctified, giving them a completed standing before God that the law never could.

basic.context: This is the summit of the letter's argument about sacrifice. The law's sacrifices, offered year after year, could never actually take away sins; they only reminded people of them. They were a shadow of the real thing. Then Christ came, saying in the words of Psalm 40 that God desired not sacrifices but obedience, and He offered His own body once for all. By that single offering He has perfected His people forever, and where there is forgiveness, no more sacrifice is needed.
basic.hermeneutics: Watch the contrast between 'again and again' and 'once for all', and between the priests who stand and Christ who sat down. The quotation of Psalm 40 supplies the logic: God always wanted obedience, fulfilled in Christ's willing self-offering.
basic.originalLanguages: The law is a skia, shadow. By Christ's offering we are hagiazō, sanctified, ephapax, once for all. By a single offering He has teleioō, perfected, those being sanctified.
basic.typology: The whole sacrificial system, the daily offerings and the Day of Atonement, is the shadow whose substance is Christ's one offering. Psalm 40's obedient servant is fulfilled in the Son who comes to do God's will.
basic.christ: Christ is the one who came to do God's will and offered His body once for all. Unlike the priests who stand and repeat their work, He sat down, His sacrifice finished, and by one offering He perfects His people forever.
basic.god: God did not ultimately desire animal sacrifices but the obedience they pointed to, supplied by His Son. God's will is the ground of our sanctification, and God Himself declares that He will remember our sins no more.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 40 supplies the quotation. Psalm 110 gives the enthronement. Jeremiah 31 provides the forgiveness of the new covenant.
basic.oneStory: The endless sacrifices of the old covenant always testified that sin was not yet truly removed; the story reaches its goal in Christ's one offering, which takes sin away and perfects His people forever.

context.literary: The passage completes the exposition begun at 8:1, framed by the citation of Jeremiah 31 in chapter 8 and its partial repetition here. It contrasts the standing, repeating priests with the seated, finished Christ, and its conclusion in verse 18 closes the argument, leading into the exhortation of 10:19.
context.historical: The daily and annual sacrifices of the temple were still being offered as the readers wavered. The writer shows that these very repetitions proved their inadequacy, and that Christ's single sacrifice has rendered them unnecessary.
context.canonical: The passage is a cornerstone of the doctrine of the finished, sufficient atonement, reading Psalm 40 messianically and combining it with Psalm 110 and Jeremiah 31 to declare that Christ's one offering perfects believers and ends sacrifice for sin.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To prove that the law's repeated sacrifices could never remove sin or perfect the worshiper, and that Christ's single, willing self-offering has done both, so that no further sacrifice for sin is needed.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage is doctrinal exposition; its practical force, drawn out next, is that believers, perfected and forgiven, should draw near to God with confidence and hold fast, resting in a finished work.

debates.question: How can believers be 'perfected forever' and yet 'being sanctified' at the same time?

views.label: A completed standing with an ongoing process
views.case: Verse 14 holds together a perfect, finished standing before God given once by Christ's offering, and an ongoing work of being made holy in daily life; the one secures the other.

views.label: Perfected in conscience and access, growing in holiness
views.case: The perfecting refers chiefly to the cleansed conscience and settled access to God, while sanctification is the continuing transformation into holiness, both flowing from the single offering.

debates.bearing: Both affirm that our acceptance before God rests wholly on Christ's finished work, even as His Spirit continues to make us holy; assurance and growth are not at odds.

study.originalLanguages: The argument turns on a few decisive words. The law is a skia, a shadow, not the eikōn or true form of the realities. Its sacrifices, offered again and again, could never take sins away. In contrast, by God's will believers have been hagiazō, sanctified, set apart and made holy, dia tēs prosphoras, through the offering of the body of Jesus Christ, ephapax, once for all. And verse 14 crowns it: by a single offering He has teleioō, perfected, brought to complete standing before God, tous hagiazomenous, those who are being sanctified. The perfect tense and the present participle together hold a finished standing and an ongoing work in one breath.

typology.fulfillment: A shadow whose reality is Christ's single, sufficient offering that truly takes away sins.
Fulfilled in Christ, who came saying, I have come to do Your will, offering His prepared body.

typology.type: The Day of Atonement's annual reminder of sin
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled and ended in Christ, after whose offering God remembers sins no more.

study.christ: Christ is the willing servant of Psalm 40 who came into the world declaring that God desired not endless sacrifices but obedience, and who offered the body God prepared for Him. By that single offering, done in obedience to God's will, He has sanctified His people and perfected them forever. Unlike the priests who stand day after day offering sacrifices that can never take away sins, this Priest, having made one sacrifice for sins, sat down at God's right hand, His work complete, now waiting until His enemies are made His footstool. In Him sin is truly taken away and the worshiper is fully fitted to draw near to God.
study.god: God is revealed as the one who never ultimately desired the blood of bulls and goats but the obedience those sacrifices foreshadowed, an obedience supplied at last by His Son. God's own will is the ground of our sanctification: it is by that will, accomplished in Christ's offering, that we are made holy. And God speaks the final word of the new covenant Himself, declaring through the Spirit that He will remember our sins and lawless acts no more, so that where He has forgiven, no further offering for sin is needed.

crossReferences.note: Sacrifice You did not desire, but a body You prepared for Me, quoted as Christ's words in verses 5 to 7.
He sat down and waits for His enemies to be made a footstool, applied to the finished sacrifice.
The new covenant promise of inward law and sins remembered no more, cited in verses 16 to 17.
Christ suffered once for sins, the righteous for the unrighteous, matching the once-for-all offering.

study.oneStory: For centuries the altars of Israel ran with blood, and the very repetition of the sacrifices confessed that sin was not yet taken away. The whole system was a shadow, waiting. When Christ came into the world, He came to do God's will, offering the body prepared for Him once for all. That single sacrifice perfects His people forever and secures the forgiveness God promised through Jeremiah. The story of sacrifice ends here: sin is taken away, and no further offering will ever be needed.

quiz.q: Why could the law's sacrifices never truly deal with sin?

options.text: They were only a shadow, and it is impossible for the blood of bulls and goats to take away sins.
They were offered by the wrong people.
options.note: The problem was not the personnel but the inability of animal blood to remove sins.

options.text: They had to be repeated endlessly, year after year.
options.note: Their constant repetition did expose their weakness, but the root reason is that animal blood simply cannot take away sins.

options.text: They actually did take away sins fully.
options.note: The passage says they could not take away sins.

quiz.q: What did Christ say, in the words of Psalm 40, when He came into the world?

options.text: Sacrifice You did not desire, but a body You prepared for Me; I have come to do Your will.
That He came to do God's will, fulfilling what was written of Him.
options.note: This is true and part of the quotation, whose fuller point is that God desired obedience, not sacrifice, and a body was prepared for Christ.

options.text: That God delights in burnt offerings above all.
options.note: The quotation says God took no delight in them.

options.text: That He came only to teach.
options.note: He came to do God's will by offering His body, not merely to teach.

quiz.q: What has Christ accomplished by His single offering?

options.text: He has perfected forever those who are being sanctified.
He has sanctified His people through the offering of His body.
options.note: True, from verse 10, and verse 14 adds that by that single offering He has perfected them forever.

options.text: He has left the work unfinished.
options.note: He sat down, His sacrifice complete.

options.text: He has perfected only those who never sin again.
options.note: He perfects those being sanctified, who are still growing in holiness.

quiz.q: What follows from God's promise to remember our sins no more?

options.text: Where there is forgiveness, an offering for sin is no longer needed.
We must keep offering sacrifices to be safe.
options.note: No further offering is needed once sins are forgiven.
The forgiveness is settled; God remembers our sins no more.

options.text: Our sins are fully forgiven, though we still confess them.
options.note: Forgiveness is complete, which is the point; the verse draws the further conclusion that no offering for sin is any longer needed.

questions: Christ has perfected forever those being sanctified. How does holding a finished standing before God together with ongoing growth free you from both pride and despair?
The priests stood and repeated their work; Christ sat down. What does His sitting down tell you about the completeness of His work for you?
God says He will remember your sins no more. Do you live as one truly forgiven, or do you keep re-offering for sins God has already forgotten?
Christ came to do God's will. How does His willing obedience shape your own obedience to the Father?
No further offering for sin is needed. What self-made sacrifices or penances might you be adding to Christ's finished work?

translationNotes.note: The quotation of Psalm 40 reads 'a body You prepared for Me', following the Septuagint; the Hebrew reads 'ears You have opened for Me', both pointing to wholehearted obedience.
'Made perfect for all time those who are being sanctified' holds a completed perfect tense and an ongoing present participle together, uniting a finished standing with a continuing work.
'Shadow of the good things to come, not the realities themselves' contrasts skia, shadow, with the true form; the law sketches what only Christ supplies.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Hebrews 10:1-18. No em dashes occurred in this passage. Sermon set to none; no expositor series confirmed this session.
